refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total evaluation time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement: remote operators must reliably command and receive feedback from equipment. The 0.999 threshold (approximately 43 minutes downtime per month) is a standard for safety-critical teleoperation. Refinement clarifies measurement scope (bidirectional link), evaluation period (customer-defined), and the quantitative target.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state transitions (up/down) during the evaluation period; automated logging of outage duration and root cause; statistical analysis of availability = (total_time - total_downtime) / total_time.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command link from tele-operator to vehicle.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional telemetry link from vehicle to tele-operator.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall detect link failures and initiate failover or safe shutdown within defined time bounds.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall log and report link availability metrics to support verification against the 0.999 target.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Redundant network paths (primary + backup) with automatic failover; dual-modem architecture; geographic diversity of network providers.
DP-B: Single high-reliability network path with aggressive error correction and retransmission; QoS prioritization; local caching of critical commands.
DP-C: Hybrid approach combining redundant paths (DP-A) with local autonomy fallback; vehicle can execute pre-loaded mission segments if link is lost; operator can resume control when link recovers.
DP-D: Mesh network topology with multiple relay nodes; peer-to-peer link establishment; distributed link state consensus.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Hybrid redundancy with local autonomy fallback. Implement dual-modem teleoperation with automatic failover between primary and backup network paths; equip vehicle with onboard mission planning and autonomous execution capability to maintain productivity during transient link loss; implement link state monitoring and operator notification; log all link events for availability verification.

potential_sources: DP-C couples teleoperation link availability to vehicle autonomy capability (FR-NGHE-010 couples to autonomous mission execution FR). This is acceptable because autonomy is a separate system concern and the coupling is unidirectional: link availability does not depend on autonomy, but autonomy enhances resilience.
DP-C couples to network infrastructure selection (primary/backup provider choice). This is a design decision that must be made in coordination with Safety Services and Site Manager stakeholder input.
coupling_check.notes: DP-C is preferred over DP-A (pure redundancy) because it adds operational value during link loss. DP-B is rejected because single-path architectures cannot reliably achieve 0.999 availability without extraordinary network SLA guarantees. DP-D (mesh) is rejected as over-complex for a point-to-point teleoperation scenario and introduces additional failure modes.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Customer-defined evaluation period is ≥ 30 days; shorter periods may not provide statistically significant availability data.
Assumption: Network providers can deliver ≥ 0.9999 availability on individual paths; if not, redundancy alone cannot achieve 0.999 composite availability.
Risk: Failover latency (time to detect failure + time to switch to backup) may cause transient command loss; must be quantified and acceptable to operator.
Risk: Vehicle autonomy fallback may not be acceptable for all mission types (e.g., precision demolition); operational policies must define when autonomy is permitted.
Risk: Dual-modem hardware cost and power consumption increase; must be evaluated against mission duration and vehicle power budget.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-C to explicitly specify: (1) failover detection time (e.g., 2–5 seconds), (2) acceptable autonomy fallback mission types (e.g., hold position, return to base, continue pre-planned path), (3) network provider SLA targets for each path, (4) link state logging format and availability calculation method.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the bidirectional command and telemetry link between tele-operator and vehicle, including modem hardware, link state management, failover logic, and link availability monitoring. The module interfaces with the Vehicle for command execution and telemetry collection, and with Safety Services for link failure alerts.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Tele-operator sends command messages (movement, tool control, mission parameters) to Teleoperation module; Teleoperation forwards to Vehicle.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle sends sensor data, position, status, and link health metrics to Teleoperation module; Teleoperation forwards to tele-operator display and logging systems.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module sends link failure alerts to Safety Services; Safety Services triggers safe shutdown or operator notification.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module manages primary and backup network connections (dual modems); Network Link represents the physical/logical network paths to external network providers.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of link availability logic. Dual-modem failover, link state monitoring, and availability logging are core to this module. Minor diffusion: Vehicle must implement link-loss autonomy fallback (owned by Vehicle), and Safety Services must implement link-failure response (owned by Safety Services).

Vehicle could own link management hardware, but this would scatter teleoperation logic across two modules. Vehicle owns the autonomy fallback behavior, not the link availability mechanism itself. Not recommended as primary owner.

Safety Services owns the response to link failure (safe shutdown, operator alert), not the link availability mechanism. Assigning link availability to Safety Services would couple safety policy to network architecture. Not recommended.
NGHE Solution is the system-level container; it does not own specific subsystem functions. Assigning link availability here would bypass architectural decomposition. Not recommended.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the correct owner of FR-NGHE-010 (link availability). It owns the dual-modem hardware, failover logic, link state monitoring, and availability metrics. Vehicle and Safety Services are consumers of link state information and implement complementary behaviors (autonomy fallback, safe shutdown) but do not own the availability mechanism.
containment_risks: Risk: If network provider SLAs are not met, Teleoperation cannot achieve 0.999 availability regardless of internal design. Mitigation: Establish network SLA contracts before design completion; include SLA verification in gate review.
Risk: Failover latency may cause transient command loss during link switch. Mitigation: Specify maximum failover time in DP refinement; validate via testing.
Risk: Dual-modem hardware may not be available or cost-prohibitive for some vehicle platforms. Mitigation: Evaluate hardware availability and cost early; consider phased rollout if needed.
Risk: Link availability logging may consume significant storage/bandwidth. Mitigation: Define log retention policy and compression strategy in IM design.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that Teleoperation module design includes: (1) dual-modem hardware specification and driver software, (2) failover state machine with explicit timing, (3) link state database schema for availability calculation, (4) interface contracts with Vehicle (Tasking in, Telemetry out) and Safety Services (SafetyAlert out), (5) network provider SLA requirements document.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (hybrid redundancy with autonomy fallback) directly addresses FR-NGHE-010 (link availability ≥ 0.999). Dual-modem failover reduces link downtime; autonomy fallback maintains productivity during transient loss. DP-C is feasible, testable, and aligns with safety-critical teleoperation best practices. No decoupling issues identified.

diffusion_risks: Minor diffusion: Vehicle module must implement autonomy fallback behavior (pre-loaded mission execution, hold-position logic). This is acceptable because autonomy is a separate Vehicle concern; Teleoperation does not own it.

Minor diffusion: Safety Services module must implement link-failure response (operator alert, safe shutdown trigger). This is acceptable because safety policy is a Safety Services concern; Teleoperation does not own it.
Network provider SLA is external to NGHE Solution; Teleoperation depends on it but does not own it. Mitigation: Establish SLA contracts and include in project risk register.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module cleanly contains the link availability mechanism (dual modems, failover logic, monitoring). External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and do not leak internal logic. Containment score 0.95 is justified.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Link availability is directly measurable via continuous link state logging. Verification method: (1) instrument Teleoperation module to log link up/down transitions with timestamps, (2) run evaluation period (customer-defined, ≥ 30 days), (3) calculate availability = (total_time - total_downtime) / total_time, (4) compare to 0.999 target. Feasible and repeatable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: FR-NGHE-010 is allocated to Teleoperation module. Sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-010.1 through 010.4) are allocated to Teleoperation with dependencies on Vehicle (autonomy) and Safety Services (failure response). Allocation is clear and traceable.

recommended_revisions: Establish network provider SLA contracts (primary and backup paths) before detailed design; include SLA verification in project schedule.
Define failover detection time and acceptable latency in DP refinement; validate via simulation or prototype testing.
Specify autonomy fallback mission types and operational policies in coordination with Site Manager and Safety Manager stakeholders.
Create link availability monitoring and logging specification (data schema, retention policy, calculation method) as part of Teleoperation module design.
Include link availability verification test plan in system test strategy; allocate evaluation period in project schedule.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (command link, telemetry link, failure detection, metrics logging). Each sub-FR requires further design and allocation. DP-C is sound and containment in Teleoperation is justified, but the sub-FRs must be refined and allocated to specific design elements (modem drivers, failover state machine, logging service, etc.) before implementation can proceed. Recommend decomposing to next level.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command link from tele-operator to vehicle with latency ≤ [customer-defined] milliseconds.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional telemetry link from vehicle to tele-operator with update rate ≥ [customer-defined] Hz.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall detect link failures within [2–5] seconds and initiate automatic failover to backup network path or trigger safe shutdown via Safety Services.

FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall log all link state transitions (up/down, failover events, provider switch) with timestamps and calculate availability metrics for verification against 0.999 target.

assumptions: Customer-defined evaluation period is ≥ 30 days to provide statistically significant availability data.
Network providers can deliver ≥ 0.9999 availability on individual paths; if not, redundancy alone cannot achieve 0.999 composite availability.
Vehicle platform can support dual-modem hardware and autonomy fallback software.
Failover latency of 2–5 seconds is acceptable to tele-operator and does not violate safety constraints.
Autonomy fallback mission types (hold position, return to base, continue pre-planned path) are acceptable for customer's operational policies.
Link availability logging does not exceed vehicle onboard storage or network bandwidth constraints.
open_risks: Network provider SLA contracts not yet established; risk of unmet availability targets if SLAs are insufficient.
Failover latency may cause transient command loss; impact on operator workload and safety not yet quantified.
Dual-modem hardware cost and power consumption not yet evaluated against vehicle budget and mission duration.
Autonomy fallback behavior may not be acceptable for all mission types; operational policies not yet finalized.
Link availability logging storage and bandwidth requirements not yet estimated.
evidence_needed: Network provider SLA documentation (primary and backup paths) with availability guarantees.
Failover latency measurement from prototype or simulation (time to detect failure + time to switch to backup).
Vehicle hardware cost and power budget analysis for dual-modem configuration.
Operational policy document defining acceptable autonomy fallback mission types and constraints.
Link availability logging specification (data schema, retention policy, storage/bandwidth estimates).
Tele-operator workload and safety analysis for transient link loss scenarios.
System test plan for link availability verification (evaluation period, measurement method, acceptance criteria).
